title: List: ArrayList vs LinkedList
description: The List interface, its two most common implementations, and when each one's performance actually matters.
objectives: Understand List as an interface with multiple implementations
Compare ArrayList and LinkedList's performance trade-offs
Choose between them based on the actual usage pattern

sections.text: In the JCF Overview, we recommended declaring List<String> names = new ArrayList<>();. Today we look at WHY there's a choice at all -- List has more than one implementation, and ArrayList isn't always the best fit.

Both use the exact same List methods (add, get, remove) identically -- this is the whole benefit of programming against the List interface (JCF Overview) rather than a concrete class. What differs is what happens INTERNALLY, and therefore performance.

ArrayList is backed by an actual array under the hood (recall 1D Arrays) -- when it runs out of room, it silently creates a bigger array and copies everything over. This gives it fast index-based access (get(i) is nearly instant), but inserting or removing from the MIDDLE requires shifting every element after it.

LinkedList (previewed here, covered properly with the manual Node-based version in the Linked List module) stores each element in its own small node, linked to the next and previous node -- like a chain. Inserting or removing in the middle just relinks a couple of nodes, no shifting required. But finding the element at index 500 means walking the chain from the start, one link at a time.

sections.title: ArrayList vs LinkedList
rows: Slow -- must walk the chain from the start

sections.text: LinkedList also implements Deque (double-ended queue), giving it addFirst/addLast/removeFirst/removeLast methods that ArrayList doesn't have. We only preview this here -- Deque gets its own full lesson in the Stacks & Queues module.

In everyday code, ArrayList is the more common default -- most programs read/access elements far more often than they insert into the middle. Reach for LinkedList specifically when your usage pattern involves frequent middle insertions/removals, or when you need Deque-style operations at both ends.

numbers.remove(30) alone would try to remove the element AT INDEX 30 (throwing an exception, since this list has only 5 elements), because remove(int) is overloaded (Method Overloading) to mean 'remove by index.' Wrapping it as Integer.valueOf(30) forces Java to match the remove(Object) overload instead, removing the VALUE 30 -- a classic List gotcha.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Calling list.remove(30) expecting it to remove the value 30, when it actually removes the element at index 30 -- use Integer.valueOf(30) to remove by value instead.
Defaulting to LinkedList without a specific reason, when ArrayList's fast random access fits most everyday use cases better.
Assuming LinkedList's get(index) is just as fast as ArrayList's -- it must walk the chain from the start, which is much slower for large lists.
Forgetting that repeatedly inserting into the middle of a large ArrayList is slow, since every following element must shift.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the main performance difference between ArrayList and LinkedList?
questions.answer: ArrayList offers fast index-based access (get(i)) since it's backed by an array, but inserting/removing in the middle is slow due to shifting elements. LinkedList offers fast middle insertion/removal since it just relinks nodes, but get(i) is slow since it must walk the chain from the start.

questions.question: When would you choose LinkedList over ArrayList?
questions.answer: When the usage pattern involves frequent insertions or removals in the middle of the list, or when Deque-style operations (adding/removing from both ends) are needed -- otherwise, ArrayList is usually the better default.

questions.question: Why does list.remove(30) behave differently than list.remove(Integer.valueOf(30))?
questions.answer: remove is overloaded: remove(int index) removes by position, while remove(Object o) removes by matching value. A plain int argument like 30 matches the index version; wrapping it as Integer.valueOf(30) forces the value-based overload instead.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: List is an interface with multiple implementations -- ArrayList and LinkedList are the two most common. ArrayList is backed by a resizable array, giving fast index-based access but slow middle insertion/removal (since elements must shift). LinkedList is a chain of linked nodes, giving fast middle insertion/removal (just relinking neighbors) but slow index-based access (must walk the chain). ArrayList is the sensible everyday default; LinkedList suits frequent middle modifications or Deque-style use at both ends. A classic gotcha: list.remove(30) removes by INDEX, while list.remove(Integer.valueOf(30)) removes by VALUE, due to overloading.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: ArrayList = a numbered row of seats -- jump straight to any seat, but squeezing a new one into the middle means everyone shifts. LinkedList = a human chain holding hands -- inserting someone just means two people let go and grab the new person instead, but finding person #500 means walking the whole chain.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Which is faster for get(index): ArrayList or LinkedList? Why?
Which is faster for inserting into the middle: ArrayList or LinkedList? Why?
When would you specifically choose LinkedList?
Why does list.remove(30) behave differently than list.remove(Integer.valueOf(30))?
